statement.markdown: Given the head of a linked list, return the length of the cycle in the linked list. If no cycle is present, return 0. The linked list contains at least one node, and all the values are unique. The linked list is guaranteed to be non-empty and the tail of the linked list connects to the node where the cycle begins. The number of nodes in the linked list will not exceed 10000.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains the number of nodes in the linked list. The second line contains the values of the nodes in the linked list. The third line contains the index of the node where the cycle begins (0-indexed).
statement.outputFormat: The length of the cycle in the linked list. If no cycle is present, return 0.

constraints.description: number of nodes in the linked list
values of the nodes in the linked list
index of the node where the cycle begins

examples.explanation: The cycle begins at the node with value 3 and has a length of 5 (3 -> 4 -> 5 -> 6 -> 7 -> 3).
There is no cycle in the linked list.

hints.text: consider using Floyd's Tortoise and Hare algorithm to detect the cycle in the linked list
initialize two pointers, one moving twice as fast as the other, to traverse the linked list
when the fast pointer meets the slow pointer, it means a cycle is present, so reset the slow pointer to the head and keep the fast pointer at the meeting point
now, move both pointers one step at a time and they will meet at the start of the cycle
the length of the cycle is the difference between the meeting point and the start of the cycle, which can be found by counting the steps between the meeting point and the start of the cycle

editorial.title: Detecting Cycle Length in Linked Lists
editorial.intuition: The problem of finding the length of a loop in a linked list can be approached by first detecting whether a cycle exists. A brute force method would involve traversing the list and checking every node against every other node, which is inefficient. A more intuitive approach involves using two pointers moving at different speeds to detect the cycle, leveraging the fact that they will eventually meet if a cycle exists.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach to solving this problem involves checking every node against every other node to see if any node points back to a previous node, indicating a cycle. This method is highly inefficient due to its quadratic time complexity, making it impractical for large lists.
editorial.betterSolution: An improvement over the brute force method is to use a hash set to store the nodes as we traverse the list. If we encounter a node that is already in the set, we know a cycle exists. However, this approach still requires additional space proportional to the number of nodes.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution utilizes Floyd's Tortoise and Hare algorithm, also known as the slow and fast pointer technique. This method involves two pointers, one moving twice as fast as the other. If a cycle exists, these two pointers will eventually meet at some node within the cycle. After detecting the cycle, we can find the start of the cycle and then the length of the cycle by moving one pointer around the cycle while keeping the other pointer at the meeting point, counting the number of steps until they meet again.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by understanding that if a cycle exists, the fast pointer will eventually catch up to the slow pointer within the cycle due to the difference in their speeds. Once the meeting point is found, resetting one pointer to the start and moving both at the same speed guarantees they will meet at the start of the cycle. Then, moving one pointer around the cycle while keeping the other at the start of the cycle allows us to count the length of the cycle accurately.
editorial.dryRun: To ensure the solution works as expected, a dry run can be performed with a sample linked list that contains a cycle. This involves manually tracing the steps of the algorithm and verifying that the cycle length is correctly identified.
commonMistakes: Not handling the case where the cycle index is -1, indicating no cycle.
Incorrectly implementing the slow and fast pointer technique.
Failing to reset the slow pointer to the head of the list after the cycle is detected.
Not counting the cycle length correctly after finding the start of the cycle.
Not considering edge cases such as an empty list or a list with a single node.
editorial.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the number of nodes, because Floyd's algorithm traverses each node at most a constant number of times.
editorial.spaceComplexity: O(1) additional space, only a few pointers are used regardless of list size.

solutions.python:
import sys

class ListNode:
    __slots__ = ('val', 'next')
    def __init__(self, val=0, next=None):
        self.val = val
        self.next = next

class Solution:
    def lengthOfCycle(self, head: ListNode) -> int:
        # Floyd's cycle detection
        slow = fast = head
        while fast and fast.next:
            slow = slow.next
            fast = fast.next.next
            if slow is fast:
                # cycle found, compute its length
                cnt = 1
                cur = slow.next
                while cur is not slow:
                    cnt += 1
                    cur = cur.next
                return cnt
        return 0

if __name__ == '__main__':
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    if not data:
        sys.exit(0)
    it = iter(data)
    n = int(next(it))
    values = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(n)]
    cycle_index = int(next(it))
    # build linked list
    nodes = [ListNode(v) for v in values]
    for i in range(n - 1):
        nodes[i].next = nodes[i + 1]
    head = nodes[0] if nodes else None
    if 0 <= cycle_index < n:
        nodes[-1].next = nodes[cycle_index]
    # solve
    sol = Solution()
    ans = sol.lengthOfCycle(head)
    print(ans)
